does: This paper proposes a bi-level adversarial training framework for defending language models against unseen jailbreak prompts through activation steering. The core method simulates diverse jailbroken activations by extrapolating from refusal-state harmful-request activations using unsupervised latent direction discovery, thereby expanding coverage of real jailbreak activation subspaces without requiring labeled jailbreak data. In the inner optimization loop, the approach generates adversarial jailbroken states through directional extrapolation; in the outer loop, it trains a potential-induced steering field to push these adversarial activations toward refusal regions while preserving benign model behavior. The framework operates at test time through activation interventions and demonstrates improved robustness across multiple language models and safety benchmarks compared to supervised steering baselines.
connects: This work builds on the emerging area of safety steering through test-time activation intervention, which emerged as an alternative to retraining or fine-tuning for aligning large language models. It extends prior supervised steering methods that rely on limited, fixed training sets of jailbreak examples by addressing their fundamental limitation: distribution shift when encountering novel attacks. The paper connects to broader adversarial training literature in machine learning, adapting the paradigm of generating adversarial examples during training to the novel setting of unsupervised latent space exploration. It also draws on recent work in mechanistic interpretability and activation analysis that reveals how harmful behaviors are encoded in model hidden states, enabling targeted intervention without modifying weights.
enables: This approach opens pathways toward more robust and adaptive safety mechanisms that can generalize to novel jailbreak attacks without requiring constant retraining. The unsupervised latent direction discovery method could extend beyond safety to other interpretability and control applications where discovering meaningful activation directions is valuable. The framework potentially enables deployment of safer models in adversarial environments where attackers continuously develop new jailbreak strategies, reducing the need for manual annotation of emerging attacks. Future work could explore whether the discovered latent directions correspond to interpretable safety concepts, investigate how to dynamically update steering fields as new jailbreak types emerge, or apply similar bi-level adversarial training to other alignment challenges in language models.
